questions.q: What is the key difference between static and dynamic type checking?
options: Static checking happens at compile time; dynamic checking happens at runtime
Static typing is used for integer operations; dynamic typing is for string operations
Static systems are faster; dynamic systems are slower
Static checking prevents all errors; dynamic checking catches only some errors
questions.explanation: Static type checking verifies types before execution; dynamic checking enforces types during execution. Type specificity (integers vs strings) is not the distinction; performance varies; dynamic checking still catches errors.

options: Automatically deducing variable types from their usage context without explicit annotations
Checking that all variables are assigned before being used
Converting one type to another automatically
Verifying that function parameters match their declared types
questions.explanation: Type inference deduces types from context and assignment, reducing explicit annotations. Use-before-assignment is initialization checking; automatic conversion is type coercion; parameter verification is type checking.

questions.q: What is polymorphism in type systems?
options: Allowing a function or value to work with multiple types
Checking that all type declarations match their usage exactly
Converting between different representation formats
Applying the same algorithm to different data structures
questions.explanation: Polymorphism enables code to operate on multiple types (parametric or ad-hoc). Exact matching is type rigidity; conversion is coercion; algorithmic reuse is implementation, not a type system property.

options: Allowing functions to be written once and work with any type that satisfies certain constraints
Requiring each function to handle all possible input types
Checking that parameters are provided in the correct order
Using different algorithms depending on the parameter types at runtime
questions.explanation: Parametric polymorphism uses type parameters (generics), allowing one implementation for many types. Handling all types at once is impossible; parameter order is syntax; runtime selection is ad-hoc polymorphism.

questions.q: What is ad-hoc polymorphism (also called overloading)?
options: Allowing functions with the same name to have different implementations for different types
Using the same function name for unrelated purposes in different modules
Automatically converting types when necessary during execution
Applying type parameters to generic function definitions
questions.explanation: Ad-hoc polymorphism (overloading) provides type-specific implementations with one name. Same-name unrelated functions are poor practice; automatic conversion is coercion; type parameters are parametric polymorphism.

options: A type system where well-typed programs cannot encounter type errors at runtime
A type system with no unsupported operations
A type system where all types must be explicitly declared
A type system optimized for maximum execution speed
questions.explanation: Sound types guarantee type safety: well-typed programs won't fail type checks at runtime. Supported operations, explicit declaration, and speed are separate type system properties.

questions.q: What is the relationship between type completeness and expressiveness?
options: More expressive type systems can represent finer-grained guarantees but become harder to check
Expressive types make programs shorter but reduce type safety
Complete types eliminate all runtime errors completely
Expressiveness and completeness are unrelated concepts
questions.explanation: Richer type systems express more properties but increase complexity for type checking. Type expressiveness doesn't inherently affect program length or runtime errors; these properties are related in practice.

questions.q: What is a subtype relation in type systems?
options: A type relationship where a value of one type can be safely used wherever another type is expected
A way to organize type names hierarchically by length or alphabetically
A mechanism to avoid type errors in arithmetic operations
A constraint that forces all types to inherit from a common base
questions.explanation: Subtyping enables substitutability: if S is a subtype of T, S values can be used as T values. It's not about naming, arithmetic, or forced inheritance but about behavioral compatibility.

questions.q: What is a type error in a strongly-typed language?
options: Using a value in a context requiring a different, incompatible type
Assigning a value to a variable of the same declared type
Using a function that hasn't been declared yet
Performing arithmetic with numbers of different sizes
questions.explanation: Type errors occur when operations violate type constraints. Assigning to same-type variables is correct; undeclared functions are name errors; different number sizes are supported in most languages.

questions.q: What is the Hindley-Milner type inference algorithm primarily used for?
options: Automatically inferring types in languages like ML and Haskell without explicit annotations
Checking that type declarations match their implementations
Converting dynamic types to static types at runtime
Organizing type hierarchies in object-oriented languages
questions.explanation: Hindley-Milner enables polymorphic type inference, widely used in functional languages for deducing types automatically. Declaration matching is different; dynamic-to-static conversion is not what it does; it's not specific to OO type hierarchies.
